1.synthesis: Psalm 140 is a Davidic lament structured by the contrast between the malicious speech and plotting of evildoers and the protection and advocacy of YHWH. The opening petition is direct: “Deliver me, O Lord, from evil men; preserve me from violent men, who plan evil things in their heart and stir up wars continually. They make their tongue sharp as a serpent’s, and under their lips is the venom of asps” (Ps 140:1–3). The prayer for divine protection follows: “Guard me, O Lord, from the hands of the wicked; preserve me from violent men, who have planned to trip up my feet” (140:4). The psalmist then expresses confidence in YHWH’s covenantal advocacy: “I know that the Lord will maintain the cause of the afflicted, and will execute justice for the needy” (140:12). The psalm closes with the assurance of righteous dwelling in YHWH’s presence.
1.historical_context: The serpent-tongue imagery of Psalm 140:3 is drawn from the ancient Near Eastern association between snake venom and malicious speech—words that wound invisibly, that spread through a community like poison, that kill before the victim can identify the source. The asp venom under the lips echoes Romans 3:13’s citation of this verse in Paul’s universal indictment of sin. The “net and cords” and “traps” set by the wicked (140:5) extend the hunting metaphor of entrapment that runs through many lament psalms: the psalmist is prey to those who use concealed devices rather than open confrontation. The advocacy of YHWH for the afflicted and needy (140:12) grounds the petition in the covenantal structure: YHWH’s justice is not merely general but specifically oriented toward those who are vulnerable and without human advocates.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 140 is organized by the alternation between description of the enemy (vv. 1–3, 5), petition for protection (vv. 4, 6), prayer for YHWH’s judgment on the wicked (vv. 8–11), and expression of confidence (vv. 12–13). The central confidence-declaration of verse 12 functions as the psalm’s theological pivot: knowing that YHWH maintains the cause of the afflicted transforms the preceding petitions from desperate pleading into prayer by one who already knows the outcome. The closing verse—“surely the righteous shall give thanks to your name; the upright shall dwell in your presence” (140:13)—shifts to the third person and the future, so that the psalm ends with a communal vision of YHWH’s presence as the righteous’ dwelling place rather than a request for personal safety.
1.theological_application: The serpent-tongue imagery (140:3) names malicious speech as a form of violence: the psalmist does not merely complain about unkind words but identifies the speech of the wicked as a weapon with venom, as dangerous as physical attack. Communities shaped by this psalm take speech seriously as a moral category—the tongue that sharpens itself against another person carries the character of the serpent of Genesis, and the prayer for deliverance from such speech is a prayer for deliverance from real harm. The confidence of verse 12—that YHWH maintains the cause of the afflicted—is the foundation on which the lament can rest without becoming despair: the psalmist is not the only advocate for his own cause; YHWH himself is his attorney before the court of ultimate justice.
1.christology: The venom of asps under the lips of the wicked (140:3) is cited by Paul in Romans 3:13 as evidence of universal human sinfulness—making the psalm not merely a prayer against specific enemies but a description of the fallen human condition from which Christ delivers. Christ himself was surrounded by those who plotted against him with malicious speech, as the Gospels record: “they came to trap him in his words” (Matt 22:15). YHWH’s advocacy for the afflicted (140:12) is concentrated in Christ’s own advocacy: “we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous” (1 John 2:1). The upright dwelling in YHWH’s presence (140:13) is the promise of those who are righteous in Christ: “we shall always be with the Lord” (1 Thess 4:17).
